expanded_description: A comprehensive exploration of double displacement (replacement) reactions, where two ionic compounds exchange ions to form two new compounds, guided by solubility rules that predict precipitate formation, neutralization reactions, and gas-forming reactions.

core_concept.principle: Double displacement reactions follow the pattern $AB + CD \rightarrow AD + CB$, where two ionic compounds exchange ions to form two new compounds, often producing a precipitate, water, or gas that drives the reaction forward.
core_concept.explanation: In double displacement reactions, the positive ions (cations) and negative ions (anions) from two different compounds switch partners. These reactions typically occur in aqueous solution where ions are free to move. The reaction proceeds when one of the products is removed from solution as a precipitate (insoluble solid), water (in neutralization), or gas. Solubility rules predict which ionic compounds are insoluble and will form precipitates. These reactions are essential in water treatment, qualitative analysis, and industrial chemical production.

image.alt: Diagram showing two ionic compounds exchanging ions to form two new compounds
image.caption: Double displacement pattern: $AB + CD \rightarrow AD + CB$; cations and anions exchange partners.

sections.introduction: Double displacement reactions, also called metathesis reactions, are fundamental chemical processes where two ionic compounds exchange ions to form new compounds. These reactions are driven by the formation of an insoluble product that removes ions from solution.

key_concept.definition: A double displacement reaction is a chemical reaction in which two ionic compounds exchange ions to form two new compounds, following the general pattern $AB + CD \rightarrow AD + CB$, where A and C are cations, B and D are anions.
key_concept.context: Double displacement reactions typically occur in aqueous solution where ionic compounds dissociate into free ions. The reaction proceeds when one product is insoluble (precipitate), is water (neutralization), or is a gas. These are not redox reactions—oxidation states don't change.

image.alt: Diagram showing ion exchange in aqueous solution with precipitate formation
image.caption: Ion exchange: cations and anions switch partners in aqueous solution; precipitate drives reaction.

classifications.value: Two aqueous ionic compounds form an insoluble solid precipitate
characteristics: Both reactants are aqueous (dissolved in water)
classifications.behavior: Ions in solution collide and form insoluble compound that precipitates out. Solubility rules predict which combinations form precipitates. Used in qualitative analysis to identify ions.

examples.explanation: $\text{AgNO}_3(aq) + \text{NaCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s)\downarrow + \text{NaNO}_3(aq)$. White $\text{AgCl}$ precipitate forms immediately. $\text{Ag}^+$ and $\text{Cl}^-$ combine; $\text{Na}^+$ and $\text{NO}_3^-$ remain in solution (spectator ions).

image.alt: White AgCl precipitate forming from clear solutions
image.caption: $\text{AgNO}_3 + \text{NaCl} \rightarrow \text{AgCl}\downarrow + \text{NaNO}_3$: white precipitate indicates reaction.

examples.explanation: $\text{Pb}(\text{NO}_3)_2(aq) + 2\text{KI}(aq) \rightarrow \text{PbI}_2(s)\downarrow + 2\text{KNO}_3(aq)$. Bright yellow $\text{PbI}_2$ precipitate forms. Classic 'golden rain' demonstration. $\text{Pb}^{2+}$ and $\text{I}^-$ combine.

image.alt: Yellow PbI2 precipitate forming
image.caption: $\text{Pb}(\text{NO}_3)_2 + 2\text{KI} \rightarrow \text{PbI}_2\downarrow + 2\text{KNO}_3$: yellow precipitate.

examples.explanation: $\text{BaCl}_2(aq) + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4(aq) \rightarrow \text{BaSO}_4(s)\downarrow + 2\text{NaCl}(aq)$. White $\text{BaSO}_4$ precipitate forms. Used to test for sulfate ions. $\text{BaSO}_4$ is very insoluble.

image.alt: White BaSO4 precipitate forming
image.caption: $\text{BaCl}_2 + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{BaSO}_4\downarrow + 2\text{NaCl}$: sulfate test.

classifications.value: Acid + base react to form water and salt
classifications.behavior: $\text{H}^+$ from acid combines with $\text{OH}^-$ from base to form $\text{H}_2\text{O}$. Remaining ions form salt. Used in antacids, soil treatment, industrial waste neutralization.

examples.explanation: $\text{HCl}(aq) + \text{NaOH}(aq) \rightarrow \text{NaCl}(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. Strong acid + strong base. Heat released (exothermic). Net ionic: $\text{H}^+ + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$.

image.alt: Acid-base neutralization with temperature increase
image.caption: $\text{HCl} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$: neutralization releases heat.

examples.explanation: $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4(aq) + 2\text{KOH}(aq) \rightarrow \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4(aq) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. Diprotic acid requires 2 base equivalents. Forms potassium sulfate salt. Used in titrations.

image.alt: Sulfuric acid titration with KOH
image.caption: $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{KOH} \rightarrow \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$: diprotic acid neutralization.

examples.explanation: $\text{Mg}(\text{OH})_2(s) + 2\text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2(aq) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. Milk of magnesia neutralizes excess stomach acid ($\text{HCl}$). Relieves heartburn and indigestion.

image.alt: Antacid tablet neutralizing acid
image.caption: $\text{Mg}(\text{OH})_2 + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$: antacid action.

classifications.value: Reaction produces a gas that escapes from solution
characteristics: Often involves carbonates, sulfites, or sulfides
classifications.behavior: Acid reacts with carbonate/sulfite/sulfide to form unstable intermediate that decomposes to gas. Gas escapes, removing product from solution, driving reaction to completion.

examples.explanation: $2\text{HCl}(aq) + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3(aq) \rightarrow 2\text{NaCl}(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{CO}_3(aq)$. $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$ decomposes: $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2\uparrow$. Overall: $2\text{HCl} + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightarrow 2\text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2\uparrow$.

image.alt: Bubbles of CO2 forming from acid + carbonate
image.caption: Acid + carbonate $\rightarrow \text{CO}_2\uparrow$: gas formation drives reaction.

examples.explanation: $2\text{HCl}(aq) + \text{Na}_2\text{S}(aq) \rightarrow 2\text{NaCl}(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{S}\uparrow$. $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ gas has rotten egg smell. Toxic gas—perform in fume hood. Used to identify sulfide ions.

image.alt: H2S gas formation from acid + sulfide
image.caption: Acid + sulfide $\rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{S}\uparrow$: toxic gas with rotten egg smell.

examples.explanation: $\text{NaHCO}_3(s) + \text{CH}_3\text{COOH}(aq) \rightarrow \text{NaCH}_3\text{COO}(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) + \text{CO}_2\uparrow$. Common household reaction. $\text{CO}_2$ bubbles used in volcanoes, cleaning. Safe, visible gas formation.

image.alt: Baking soda and vinegar reaction with bubbles
image.caption: $\text{NaHCO}_3 + \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2\uparrow$: household gas-forming reaction.

principles.statement: Solubility rules determine which ionic compounds are insoluble and will form precipitates in double displacement reactions.
principles.application: Memorize key rules: All nitrates soluble; all Group 1 salts soluble; most chlorides soluble except $\text{Ag}^+$, $\text{Pb}^{2+}$, $\text{Hg}_2^{2+}$; most sulfates soluble except $\text{Ba}^{2+}$, $\text{Pb}^{2+}$, $\text{Ca}^{2+}$; most carbonates/hydroxides insoluble except Group 1.
principles.example: $\text{AgNO}_3 + \text{NaCl}$: $\text{AgCl}$ insoluble (exception) $\rightarrow$ precipitate. $\text{NaNO}_3 + \text{KCl}$: all products soluble $\rightarrow$ no reaction.

image.alt: Solubility rules chart showing soluble and insoluble compounds
image.caption: Solubility rules: predict which ionic compounds form precipitates.

principles.statement: Double displacement reactions proceed when a product is removed from solution as precipitate, water, or gas, shifting equilibrium toward products.
principles.application: Precipitate: insoluble solid forms ($\text{AgCl}\downarrow$). Water: neutralization ($\text{H}^+ + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$). Gas: escapes solution ($\text{CO}_2\uparrow$). Without driving force, no reaction occurs.
principles.example: $\text{NaCl} + \text{KNO}_3 \rightarrow$ no reaction (all products soluble, no driving force). $\text{AgNO}_3 + \text{NaCl} \rightarrow \text{AgCl}\downarrow + \text{NaNO}_3$ (precipitate drives reaction).

image.alt: Diagram showing three driving forces for double displacement
image.caption: Driving forces: precipitate, water, or gas formation drives double displacement.

principles.statement: Spectator ions are ions that remain unchanged in solution during double displacement; they don't participate in the actual reaction.
principles.application: Net ionic equation shows only reacting ions. Spectator ions cancel out. Example: $\text{Ag}^+(aq) + \text{NO}_3^-(aq) + \text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s) + \text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{NO}_3^-(aq)$. Net: $\text{Ag}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s)$.
principles.example: $\text{Na}^+$ and $\text{NO}_3^-$ are spectator ions in $\text{AgNO}_3 + \text{NaCl}$ reaction. They remain dissolved, unchanged. Net ionic equation omits spectators.

image.alt: Diagram showing spectator ions vs. reacting ions
image.caption: Spectator ions: remain in solution unchanged; net ionic equation omits them.

components.definition: Double displacement reaction components and patterns
components.equation: $\text{Double Displacement Pattern: } AB(aq) + CD(aq) \rightarrow AD(s/aq/g) + CB(aq/s/g)$
components.interpretation: Cations (A, C) and anions (B, D) exchange partners. Reaction occurs if AD or CB is precipitate (s), water (l), or gas (g). If both products aqueous, no reaction.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Double Displacement Reactions
worked_examples.problem: Predict if the following reactions occur. If yes, write balanced equation with states. If no, write 'no reaction': (a) $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{BaCl}_2 \rightarrow$, (b) $\text{KNO}_3 + \text{NaCl} \rightarrow$, (c) $\text{HCl} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow$.

step_by_step.calculation: Exchange ions: $\text{Na}^+$ with $\text{Cl}^-$, $\text{Ba}^{2+}$ with $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$. Products: $\text{NaCl} + \text{BaSO}_4$. Check solubility: $\text{NaCl}$ soluble, $\text{BaSO}_4$ insoluble (precipitate). Reaction occurs. Balance: $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4(aq) + \text{BaCl}_2(aq) \rightarrow 2\text{NaCl}(aq) + \text{BaSO}_4(s)\downarrow$.

Exchange ions: $\text{K}^+$ with $\text{Cl}^-$, $\text{Na}^+$ with $\text{NO}_3^-$. Products: $\text{KCl} + \text{NaNO}_3$. Check solubility: ALL nitrates soluble, ALL Group 1 salts soluble. Both products aqueous. No precipitate, water, or gas. No reaction.

Acid + base = neutralization. Exchange: $\text{H}^+$ with $\text{OH}^-$, $\text{Na}^+$ with $\text{Cl}^-$. Products: $\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{NaCl}$. Water formation drives reaction. Balance: $\text{HCl}(aq) + \text{NaOH}(aq) \rightarrow \text{NaCl}(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4(aq) + \text{BaCl}_2(aq) \rightarrow 2\text{NaCl}(aq) + \text{BaSO}_4(s)\downarrow$ (precipitate forms). (b) no reaction (all products soluble, no driving force). (c) $\text{HCl}(aq) + \text{NaOH}(aq) \rightarrow \text{NaCl}(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$ (neutralization, water forms). Key: check solubility rules to determine if reaction occurs.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Using solubility rules to predict double displacement reaction occurrence and writing balanced equations with states

worked_examples.title: Real-World Application: Water Treatment
worked_examples.problem: Hard water contains $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ ions that cause scaling. Sodium carbonate ($\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$) is added to remove $\text{Ca}^{2+}$. (a) Write the double displacement equation. (b) Explain why this works. (c) What mass of $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$ is needed to remove 100 g $\text{Ca}^{2+}$?

step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Ca}^{2+}(aq)$ from hard water + $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3(aq) \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3(s)\downarrow + 2\text{Na}^+(aq)$. Complete: $\text{CaCl}_2(aq) + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3(aq) \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3(s)\downarrow + 2\text{NaCl}(aq)$.

$\text{CaCO}_3$ is insoluble (solubility rule: most carbonates insoluble). Precipitate removes $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ from solution, preventing scale formation in pipes, water heaters. Solid $\text{CaCO}_3$ filtered out.

Molar mass $\text{Ca}^{2+} = 40.08$ g/mol. Molar mass $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 = 2(22.99) + 12.01 + 3(16.00) = 105.99$ g/mol. Ratio: 1 mol $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ : 1 mol $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$. 100 g $\text{Ca}^{2+} \times \frac{1 \text{ mol}}{40.08 \text{ g}} \times \frac{105.99 \text{ g } \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3}{1 \text{ mol}} = 264.4$ g $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) $\text{CaCl}_2(aq) + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3(aq) \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3(s)\downarrow + 2\text{NaCl}(aq)$. (b) $\text{CaCO}_3$ is insoluble precipitate; removes $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ from solution, preventing scale. (c) 100 g $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ requires 264.4 g $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$ (1:1 molar ratio). Double displacement enables water softening.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying double displacement reaction principles to water treatment and stoichiometric calculations

sections.introduction: Solubility rules are essential tools for predicting whether double displacement reactions will occur. These rules, based on experimental observations, tell us which ionic compounds are soluble (dissolve in water) and which are insoluble (form precipitates).

key_concept.definition: Solubility rules are guidelines that predict whether an ionic compound will dissolve in water (soluble, aqueous) or form a solid precipitate (insoluble) based on the ions present.
key_concept.context: Solubility rules are memorized patterns based on experimental data. They enable prediction of precipitation reactions without testing every combination. Rules have exceptions, but cover most common compounds encountered in introductory chemistry.

image.alt: Complete solubility rules chart with soluble and insoluble compounds
image.caption: Solubility rules: memorize patterns to predict precipitate formation in double displacement.

classifications.value: These ionic compounds always dissolve in water; never form precipitates
classifications.behavior: These ions never form precipitates. If both products contain only these ions, no reaction occurs. Useful for identifying spectator ions.

examples.explanation: $\text{NaCl}$, $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$, $\text{NaOH}$, $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$—all soluble. $\text{Na}^+$ never forms precipitate. $\text{Na}^+$ is always spectator ion in double displacement.

image.alt: Various sodium compounds dissolving in water
image.caption: Group 1 salts: always soluble; $\text{Na}^+$ never precipitates.

examples.explanation: $\text{AgNO}_3$, $\text{Pb}(\text{NO}_3)_2$, $\text{Ba}(\text{NO}_3)_2$—all soluble despite $\text{Ag}^+$, $\text{Pb}^{2+}$, $\text{Ba}^{2+}$ often forming precipitates. $\text{NO}_3^-$ never forms precipitate.

image.alt: Nitrate compounds dissolving in water
image.caption: All nitrates soluble: $\text{NO}_3^-$ never forms precipitate.

classifications.value: These compounds are generally soluble except for specific cation combinations
classifications.behavior: Most chlorides/sulfates dissolve, but specific cations form insoluble compounds. These exceptions are common precipitates in double displacement reactions.

examples.explanation: $\text{NaCl}$ soluble (Na is Group 1). $\text{AgCl}$ insoluble (exception). $\text{PbCl}_2$ insoluble (exception). $\text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2$ insoluble (exception). Memorize: $\text{Ag}^+$, $\text{Pb}^{2+}$, $\text{Hg}_2^{2+}$ chlorides insoluble.

image.alt: AgCl precipitate vs. NaCl solution
image.caption: Chloride exceptions: $\text{AgCl}$, $\text{PbCl}_2$, $\text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2$ insoluble.

examples.explanation: $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$ soluble (Na is Group 1). $\text{BaSO}_4$ insoluble (exception). $\text{PbSO}_4$ insoluble (exception). $\text{CaSO}_4$ slightly soluble. $\text{BaSO}_4$ used in medical imaging (barium swallow).

image.alt: BaSO4 precipitate vs. Na2SO4 solution
image.caption: Sulfate exceptions: $\text{BaSO}_4$, $\text{PbSO}_4$ insoluble.

classifications.value: These compounds are generally insoluble except for specific cation combinations
classifications.behavior: Most carbonates/hydroxides/sulfides form precipitates. Group 1 and ammonium exceptions are always soluble. These are common precipitates in double displacement.

examples.explanation: $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$ soluble (Group 1). $\text{CaCO}_3$ insoluble (limestone, chalk). $\text{BaCO}_3$ insoluble. $\text{CuCO}_3$ insoluble. Used to remove metal ions from solution.

image.alt: CaCO3 precipitate forming
image.caption: Carbonates: insoluble except Group 1 and $\text{NH}_4^+$.

examples.explanation: $\text{NaOH}$ soluble (Group 1). $\text{Mg}(\text{OH})_2$ insoluble (milk of magnesia). $\text{Fe}(\text{OH})_3$ insoluble (rust-brown). $\text{Cu}(\text{OH})_2$ insoluble (blue). Used to identify metal ions.

image.alt: Various metal hydroxide precipitates with different colors
image.caption: Hydroxides: insoluble except Group 1, $\text{Ca}^{2+}$, $\text{Sr}^{2+}$, $\text{Ba}^{2+}$.

principles.statement: Use mnemonics to remember solubility rules: 'NAG SAG' for soluble ions, 'PMS' for chloride exceptions, 'Castro Bear' for sulfate exceptions.
principles.application: NAG SAG: $\text{NO}_3^-$, $\text{Acetate}$, Group 1, $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$, $\text{Ag}^+$ (exception), Group 17. PMS: $\text{Pb}^{2+}$, $\text{Hg}_2^{2+}$, $\text{Ag}^+$ chlorides insoluble. Castro Bear: $\text{Ca}^{2+}$, $\text{Sr}^{2+}$, $\text{Ba}^{2+}$ sulfates insoluble.
principles.example: Test $\text{BaCl}_2 + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$: $\text{BaSO}_4$? Ba in 'Castro Bear' $\rightarrow$ insoluble $\rightarrow$ precipitate. $\text{NaCl}$? Na is Group 1 (NAG SAG) $\rightarrow$ soluble.

image.alt: Mnemonic chart for solubility rules
image.caption: Mnemonics: 'NAG SAG', 'PMS', 'Castro Bear' help memorize solubility rules.

principles.statement: To predict if precipitate forms: (1) Write possible products by exchanging ions, (2) Check solubility of each product using rules, (3) If either product insoluble, precipitate forms; if both soluble, no reaction.
principles.application: $\text{AgNO}_3 + \text{NaCl}$: Products $\text{AgCl} + \text{NaNO}_3$. $\text{AgCl}$? $\text{Ag}^+$ + $\text{Cl}^-$ = exception (PMS) $\rightarrow$ insoluble. $\text{NaNO}_3$? Group 1 + nitrate (NAG SAG) $\rightarrow$ soluble. Precipitate: $\text{AgCl}$.
principles.example: $\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{BaCl}_2$: Products $\text{KCl} + \text{BaSO}_4$. $\text{KCl}$? Group 1 $\rightarrow$ soluble. $\text{BaSO}_4$? Ba in 'Castro Bear' $\rightarrow$ insoluble. Precipitate: $\text{BaSO}_4$.

image.alt: Flowchart for predicting precipitate formation
image.caption: Prediction steps: exchange ions $\rightarrow$ check solubility $\rightarrow$ identify precipitate.

components.equation: $\text{Solubility: } \begin{cases} \text{Always Soluble: Group 1, } \text{NH}_4^+, \text{NO}_3^-, \text{C}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2^- \\ \text{Soluble Except: Cl}^-, \text{Br}^-, \text{I}^- \text{ (Ag}^+, \text{Pb}^{2+}, \text{Hg}_2^{2+}\text{)} \\ \text{Soluble Except: SO}_4^{2-} \text{ (Ba}^{2+}, \text{Pb}^{2+}, \text{Ca}^{2+}, \text{Sr}^{2+}\text{)} \\ \text{Insoluble Except: CO}_3^{2-}, \text{PO}_4^{3-}, \text{OH}^- \text{ (Group 1, NH}_4^+\text{)} \end{cases}$
components.interpretation: Memorize patterns: Group 1/nitrates always soluble; chlorides/sulfates usually soluble with exceptions; carbonates/hydroxides usually insoluble with exceptions. Use rules to predict precipitates.

worked_examples.title: Applying Solubility Rules to Predict Reactions
worked_examples.problem: Use solubility rules to predict if precipitate forms. If yes, identify precipitate: (a) $\text{FeCl}_3 + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow$, (b) $\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{NaNO}_3 \rightarrow$, (c) $\text{Cu}(\text{NO}_3)_2 + \text{Na}_2\text{S} \rightarrow$.

step_by_step.calculation: Exchange ions: $\text{Fe}^{3+}$ with $\text{OH}^-$, $\text{Na}^+$ with $\text{Cl}^-$. Products: $\text{Fe}(\text{OH})_3 + \text{NaCl}$. Check solubility: $\text{Fe}(\text{OH})_3$? Hydroxides insoluble except Group 1 $\rightarrow$ insoluble precipitate. $\text{NaCl}$? Group 1 $\rightarrow$ soluble. Precipitate: $\text{Fe}(\text{OH})_3$ (rust-brown).

Exchange ions: $\text{K}^+$ with $\text{NO}_3^-$, $\text{Na}^+$ with $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$. Products: $\text{KNO}_3 + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$. Check solubility: $\text{KNO}_3$? Group 1 + nitrate $\rightarrow$ soluble. $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$? Group 1 $\rightarrow$ soluble. Both soluble $\rightarrow$ no precipitate, no reaction.

Exchange ions: $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ with $\text{S}^{2-}$, $\text{Na}^+$ with $\text{NO}_3^-$. Products: $\text{CuS} + \text{NaNO}_3$. Check solubility: $\text{CuS}$? Sulfides insoluble except Group 1/2 $\rightarrow$ insoluble precipitate (black). $\text{NaNO}_3$? Group 1 + nitrate $\rightarrow$ soluble. Precipitate: $\text{CuS}$.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) $\text{Fe}(\text{OH})_3(s)\downarrow$ precipitate forms (hydroxides insoluble except Group 1). (b) no precipitate (both products soluble: Group 1 salts). (c) $\text{CuS}(s)\downarrow$ precipitate forms (sulfides insoluble except Group 1/2). Key: apply solubility rules systematically to each product.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying solubility rules to predict precipitate formation in double displacement reactions

worked_examples.title: Writing Net Ionic Equations
worked_examples.problem: Write the net ionic equation for: $\text{AgNO}_3(aq) + \text{NaCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s) + \text{NaNO}_3(aq)$. Identify spectator ions.

step_by_step.explanation: Write complete ionic equation (dissociate all aqueous compounds).
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Ag}^+(aq) + \text{NO}_3^-(aq) + \text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s) + \text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{NO}_3^-(aq)$

step_by_step.explanation: Identify spectator ions (same on both sides).
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Na}^+(aq)$ appears on both sides $\rightarrow$ spectator. $\text{NO}_3^-(aq)$ appears on both sides $\rightarrow$ spectator.

step_by_step.explanation: Cancel spectator ions from both sides.
step_by_step.calculation: Remove $\text{Na}^+(aq)$ and $\text{NO}_3^-(aq)$ from both sides.

step_by_step.explanation: Write net ionic equation (only reacting ions).
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Ag}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s)$. This shows actual chemical change.

worked_examples.final_answer: Net ionic equation: $\text{Ag}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s)$. Spectator ions: $\text{Na}^+(aq)$ and $\text{NO}_3^-(aq)$. Net ionic equation shows only ions that actually react to form precipitate; spectators remain unchanged in solution.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Writing net ionic equations by identifying and removing spectator ions

sections.section_title: Writing and Balancing Double Displacement Equations
sections.introduction: Successfully writing double displacement equations requires predicting products correctly, determining states of matter using solubility rules, and balancing the equation. This skill is essential for understanding reaction stoichiometry and predicting reaction outcomes.

key_concept.definition: Writing double displacement equations involves: (1) identifying the two ionic compounds, (2) exchanging cations and anions to predict products, (3) determining states using solubility rules, (4) writing correct formulas for products, (5) balancing the equation.
key_concept.context: Product formulas must be written correctly using ionic charges (balance to zero). State symbols indicate which product drives the reaction (precipitate, water, or gas). Balancing ensures Law of Conservation of Mass is satisfied.

image.alt: Step-by-step flowchart for writing double displacement equations
image.caption: Equation writing steps: identify compounds $\rightarrow$ exchange ions $\rightarrow$ check solubility $\rightarrow$ write formulas $\rightarrow$ balance.

principles.statement: In double displacement, cations exchange anions: $AB + CD \rightarrow AD + CB$. Write product formulas using correct ionic charges; balance cation and anion charges to zero.

image.alt: Diagram showing ion exchange in double displacement
image.caption: Product prediction: exchange cations; write formulas using charge balance.

principles.statement: Assign state symbols using solubility rules: (aq) for soluble ionic compounds, (s) for precipitates, (l) for water, (g) for gases. States indicate driving force for reaction.
principles.application: $\text{AgNO}_3(aq) + \text{NaCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s) + \text{NaNO}_3(aq)$. (s) on $\text{AgCl}$ indicates precipitate drives reaction. (aq) on $\text{NaNO}_3$ indicates it remains dissolved.
principles.example: $\text{HCl}(aq) + \text{NaOH}(aq) \rightarrow \text{NaCl}(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. (l) on $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ indicates water formation drives neutralization.

image.alt: Examples of state symbols in double displacement equations
image.caption: State symbols: (aq) soluble, (s) precipitate, (l) water, (g) gas.

principles.statement: Balance double displacement equations by: (1) write correct product formulas first, (2) count atoms/ions of each type, (3) adjust coefficients to balance, (4) verify all atoms and charges balance. Never change subscripts.
principles.application: Start with polyatomic ions (balance as units if unchanged). Balance metals next, then nonmetals. Check polyatomic ions remain intact ($\text{SO}_4^{2-}$, $\text{NO}_3^-$, etc.).
principles.example: $\text{Pb}(\text{NO}_3)_2 + \text{KI} \rightarrow$ Products: $\text{PbI}_2 + \text{KNO}_3$. Balance I: $\text{Pb}(\text{NO}_3)_2 + 2\text{KI} \rightarrow \text{PbI}_2 + \text{KNO}_3$. Balance $\text{NO}_3$: $\text{Pb}(\text{NO}_3)_2 + 2\text{KI} \rightarrow \text{PbI}_2 + 2\text{KNO}_3$. Verify: 1 Pb, 2 $\text{NO}_3$, 2 K, 2 I both sides.

image.alt: Step-by-step balancing of double displacement equation
image.caption: Balancing strategy: write products first, then balance with coefficients.

components.equation: $\text{Equation Writing: } AB(aq) + CD(aq) \xrightarrow{\text{check solubility}} AD(s/aq/g) + CB(aq) \quad \text{(balance with coefficients)}$
components.interpretation: Exchange cations and anions. Write correct product formulas using charge balance. Assign states using solubility rules. Balance with coefficients, never change subscripts.

worked_examples.title: Writing and Balancing Double Displacement Equations
worked_examples.problem: Write and balance equations for: (a) calcium chloride + sodium carbonate, (b) sulfuric acid + potassium hydroxide, (c) iron(III) nitrate + sodium hydroxide.

step_by_step.calculation: Reactants: $\text{CaCl}_2 + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$. Exchange: $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ with $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$, $\text{Na}^+$ with $\text{Cl}^-$. Products: $\text{CaCO}_3 + \text{NaCl}$. Check solubility: $\text{CaCO}_3$ insoluble (carbonate), $\text{NaCl}$ soluble. States: $\text{CaCO}_3(s) + \text{NaCl}(aq)$. Balance: $\text{CaCl}_2(aq) + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3(aq) \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3(s)\downarrow + 2\text{NaCl}(aq)$.

Reactants: $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{KOH}$. Acid + base = neutralization. Products: $\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$. Check: $\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4$ soluble (Group 1), $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ liquid. States: $\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. Balance: $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4(aq) + 2\text{KOH}(aq) \rightarrow \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4(aq) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$.

Reactants: $\text{Fe}(\text{NO}_3)_3 + \text{NaOH}$. Exchange: $\text{Fe}^{3+}$ with $\text{OH}^-$, $\text{Na}^+$ with $\text{NO}_3^-$. Products: $\text{Fe}(\text{OH})_3 + \text{NaNO}_3$. Check solubility: $\text{Fe}(\text{OH})_3$ insoluble (hydroxide), $\text{NaNO}_3$ soluble. States: $\text{Fe}(\text{OH})_3(s) + \text{NaNO}_3(aq)$. Balance: $\text{Fe}(\text{NO}_3)_3(aq) + 3\text{NaOH}(aq) \rightarrow \text{Fe}(\text{OH})_3(s)\downarrow + 3\text{NaNO}_3(aq)$.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) $\text{CaCl}_2(aq) + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3(aq) \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3(s)\downarrow + 2\text{NaCl}(aq)$. (b) $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4(aq) + 2\text{KOH}(aq) \rightarrow \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4(aq) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. (c) $\text{Fe}(\text{NO}_3)_3(aq) + 3\text{NaOH}(aq) \rightarrow \text{Fe}(\text{OH})_3(s)\downarrow + 3\text{NaNO}_3(aq)$. Key: exchange ions, write correct formulas, check solubility for states, balance with coefficients.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Writing and balancing double displacement equations with correct product formulas and state symbols

worked_examples.title: Advanced Balancing: Polyatomic Ions
worked_examples.problem: Write and balance: aluminum sulfate + sodium hydroxide. Treat polyatomic ions as units when balancing.

step_by_step.calculation: Reactants: $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + \text{NaOH}$. Exchange: $\text{Al}^{3+}$ with $\text{OH}^-$, $\text{Na}^+$ with $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$. Products: $\text{Al}(\text{OH})_3 + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$.
$\text{Al}(\text{OH})_3$? Hydroxide insoluble except Group 1 $\rightarrow$ (s). $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$? Group 1 $\rightarrow$ (aq). States: $\text{Al}(\text{OH})_3(s) + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4(aq)$.

Left: 2 Al, 3 $\text{SO}_4$, 1 Na, 1 OH. Right: 1 Al, 1 $\text{SO}_4$, 2 Na, 3 OH. Balance Al: $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow 2\text{Al}(\text{OH})_3 + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$. Balance $\text{SO}_4$: $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow 2\text{Al}(\text{OH})_3 + 3\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$. Balance Na: $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + 6\text{NaOH} \rightarrow 2\text{Al}(\text{OH})_3 + 3\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$. Verify OH: 6 OH both sides.

worked_examples.final_answer: $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3(aq) + 6\text{NaOH}(aq) \rightarrow 2\text{Al}(\text{OH})_3(s)\downarrow + 3\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4(aq)$. Key: treat polyatomic ions ($\text{SO}_4^{2-}$, $\text{OH}^-$) as units when balancing; balance Al first, then $\text{SO}_4$, then Na, verify OH.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Balancing double displacement equations with polyatomic ions treated as units

sections.section_title: Real-World Applications of Double Displacement Reactions
sections.introduction: Double displacement reactions power countless natural processes and industrial applications. Understanding these reactions enables us to harness chemical transformations for water treatment, medicine, manufacturing, and environmental protection.

key_concept.definition: Double displacement reactions are essential in water treatment (precipitation of contaminants), medicine (antacids, diagnostic tests), industry (chemical synthesis, pigment production), and environmental protection (heavy metal removal).
key_concept.context: From purifying drinking water to neutralizing acid spills, double displacement reactions have practical applications that impact health, environment, and technology. Recognizing these applications connects classroom chemistry to real-world impact.

image.alt: Collage showing double displacement applications in water treatment, medicine, and industry
image.caption: Double displacement in action: water softening, antacids, pigment production, heavy metal removal.

classifications.value: Using precipitation to remove contaminants from water
classifications.behavior: Chemicals added to water cause contaminants to precipitate as insoluble solids. Solids filtered out, leaving purified water. Used in drinking water treatment, wastewater treatment, industrial effluent treatment.

examples.explanation: $\text{Ca}^{2+}(aq) + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3(aq) \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3(s)\downarrow + 2\text{Na}^+(aq)$. Hard water $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ precipitates as $\text{CaCO}_3$. Prevents scale in pipes, water heaters. Improves soap efficiency. Common in households and industry.

image.alt: Water softening system removing calcium ions
image.caption: Water softening: $\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{CO}_3^{2-} \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3\downarrow$; prevents scale buildup.

examples.explanation: $\text{Pb}^{2+}(aq) + \text{Na}_2\text{S}(aq) \rightarrow \text{PbS}(s)\downarrow + 2\text{Na}^+(aq)$. Toxic $\text{Pb}^{2+}$ precipitates as insoluble $\text{PbS}$. Filtered out before water discharge. Protects environment from heavy metal pollution.

image.alt: Heavy metal precipitation in wastewater treatment
image.caption: Heavy metal removal: $\text{Pb}^{2+} + \text{S}^{2-} \rightarrow \text{PbS}\downarrow$; prevents toxic discharge.

examples.explanation: $3\text{Ca}^{2+}(aq) + 2\text{PO}_4^{3-}(aq) \rightarrow \text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2(s)\downarrow$. Excess phosphates cause algal blooms. Precipitated as calcium phosphate. Prevents eutrophication in lakes and rivers.

image.alt: Phosphate precipitation in wastewater treatment
image.caption: Phosphate removal: $3\text{Ca}^{2+} + 2\text{PO}_4^{3-} \rightarrow \text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2\downarrow$; prevents algal blooms.

classifications.value: Using double displacement in medicine and medical testing
classifications.behavior: Neutralization reactions relieve acid reflux. Insoluble $\text{BaSO}_4$ used in imaging (doesn't dissolve, safe). Precipitation tests identify ions in blood/urine samples.

examples.explanation: $\text{Mg}(\text{OH})_2(s) + 2\text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2(aq) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. Milk of magnesia neutralizes excess stomach $\text{HCl}$. Relieves heartburn, indigestion. Common OTC medication.

image.alt: Antacid tablet neutralizing stomach acid
image.caption: Antacids: $\text{Mg}(\text{OH})_2 + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$; relieves heartburn.

examples.explanation: Patient drinks $\text{BaSO}_4$ suspension. $\text{BaSO}_4$ insoluble (doesn't dissolve in stomach acid). Coats digestive tract, visible on X-ray. Diagnoses ulcers, tumors, blockages. Safe because $\text{BaSO}_4$ doesn't dissolve (soluble $\text{Ba}^{2+}$ is toxic).

image.alt: Barium swallow X-ray showing digestive tract
image.caption: Barium swallow: insoluble $\text{BaSO}_4$ coats digestive tract for X-ray imaging.

examples.explanation: $\text{AgNO}_3(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s)\downarrow + \text{NO}_3^-(aq)$. Add $\text{AgNO}_3$ to sample. White $\text{AgCl}$ precipitate indicates $\text{Cl}^-$. Amount of precipitate quantifies $\text{Cl}^-$ concentration. Diagnostic for kidney function, electrolyte balance.

image.alt: Chloride test showing AgCl precipitate
image.caption: Chloride test: $\text{Ag}^+ + \text{Cl}^- \rightarrow \text{AgCl}\downarrow$; quantifies chloride in samples.

classifications.value: Using double displacement to manufacture chemicals and materials
classifications.behavior: Precipitation reactions produce insoluble pigments. Saponification (soap making) is double displacement. Chemical industry uses double displacement for synthesis of various compounds.

examples.explanation: $\text{Pb}(\text{NO}_3)_2(aq) + \text{K}_2\text{CrO}_4(aq) \rightarrow \text{PbCrO}_4(s)\downarrow + 2\text{KNO}_3(aq)$. Bright yellow $\text{PbCrO}_4$ (chrome yellow) precipitate. Used in paints, pigments. Filtered, dried, processed into paint.

image.alt: Yellow lead chromate pigment precipitate
image.caption: Pigment production: $\text{Pb}(\text{NO}_3)_2 + \text{K}_2\text{CrO}_4 \rightarrow \text{PbCrO}_4\downarrow$; chrome yellow pigment.

examples.explanation: $\text{Fat/Oil} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Soap} + \text{Glycerol}$. Fat (ester) + base $\rightarrow$ soap (salt of fatty acid) + glycerol. Double displacement at molecular level. Traditional soap making process still used today.

image.alt: Soap making process showing saponification
image.caption: Soap making: fat + $\text{NaOH} \rightarrow$ soap + glycerol; double displacement reaction.

examples.explanation: $\text{NaCl} + \text{NH}_3 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{NaHCO}_3\downarrow + \text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$. $\text{NaHCO}_3$ precipitates, heated to $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$. Used in glass, detergents, water treatment. Major industrial process.

image.alt: Solvay process for sodium carbonate production
image.caption: Solvay process: precipitation of $\text{NaHCO}_3$ for $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$ production.

classifications.value: Using precipitation to clean up environmental contamination
classifications.behavior: Chemicals added to contaminated sites cause toxic ions to precipitate. Precipitates removed or stabilized. Prevents spread of contamination. Used in Superfund site cleanup, mine remediation.

examples.explanation: Mine water acidic with dissolved metals. Add lime ($\text{Ca}(\text{OH})_2$): $\text{Fe}^{2+} + 2\text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{Fe}(\text{OH})_2\downarrow$. Metals precipitate as hydroxides. Water neutralized, metals removed. Protects rivers from contamination.

image.alt: Acid mine drainage treatment facility
image.caption: Mine drainage: $\text{Fe}^{2+} + 2\text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{Fe}(\text{OH})_2\downarrow$; removes toxic metals.

examples.explanation: $\text{Cr}^{6+}$ (toxic, soluble) reduced to $\text{Cr}^{3+}$, then precipitated: $\text{Cr}^{3+} + 3\text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{Cr}(\text{OH})_3\downarrow$. Insoluble $\text{Cr}(\text{OH})_3$ removed. Used at tanneries, electroplating facilities. Prevents $\text{Cr}^{6+}$ groundwater contamination.

image.alt: Chromium remediation site showing precipitation
image.caption: Chromium cleanup: $\text{Cr}^{3+} + 3\text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{Cr}(\text{OH})_3\downarrow$; removes toxic chromium.

principles.principle: Economic Benefits of Double Displacement Applications
principles.statement: Double displacement applications provide economic benefits by recovering valuable materials, treating waste cost-effectively, and producing essential chemicals efficiently.
principles.application: Water softening: prevents \$ billions in pipe/appliance damage. Heavy metal recovery: valuable metals (Ag, Au) recovered from waste. Soap production: low-cost cleaning products from fats/oils.
principles.example: Silver recovery from photographic waste: $\text{Ag}^+ + \text{Cl}^- \rightarrow \text{AgCl}\downarrow$. Recovered Ag refined and reused. Reduces cost, environmental impact. Precious metal recovery economically viable.

image.alt: Economic comparison of precipitation treatment vs. alternatives
image.caption: Economic benefits: precipitation treatment cost-effective, recovers valuable materials.

principles.principle: Environmental Impact of Double Displacement Applications
principles.statement: Double displacement applications reduce environmental impact by removing toxic contaminants, preventing pollution, and enabling sustainable chemical production.
principles.application: Wastewater treatment prevents toxic discharge. Acid mine drainage treatment protects rivers. Phosphate removal prevents algal blooms. All contribute to environmental sustainability.
principles.example: Phosphate removal from wastewater: $3\text{Ca}^{2+} + 2\text{PO}_4^{3-} \rightarrow \text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2\downarrow$. Prevents eutrophication in lakes. Protects aquatic ecosystems. Required by environmental regulations.

image.alt: Environmental benefits of precipitation treatment
image.caption: Environmental benefits: precipitation removes contaminants, protects ecosystems.

components.interpretation: Effective double displacement applications balance reaction feasibility (solubility rules), economic value (cost savings, material recovery), and environmental benefit (pollution prevention, remediation).

worked_examples.title: Analyzing Double Displacement Applications
worked_examples.problem: For each application, identify: (a) the double displacement reaction, (b) the driving force (precipitate/water/gas), (c) economic benefit, (d) environmental benefit: (1) water softening, (2) antacid neutralization, (3) barium swallow imaging.

step_by_step.calculation: (a) Reaction: $\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{CO}_3^{2-} \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3\downarrow$. (b) Driving force: precipitate ($\text{CaCO}_3$ insoluble). (c) Economic: prevents scale damage to pipes/appliances, saves \$ billions. (d) Environmental: reduces detergent use (soft water lathers better), less phosphate pollution.

(a) Reaction: $\text{Mg}(\text{OH})_2 + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$. (b) Driving force: water formation (neutralization). (c) Economic: OTC medication, low cost, relieves discomfort. (d) Environmental: minimal (small quantities, biodegradable).

(a) Reaction: $\text{Ba}^{2+} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} \rightarrow \text{BaSO}_4\downarrow$ (prepared beforehand). (b) Driving force: precipitate ($\text{BaSO}_4$ insoluble, safe). (c) Economic: non-invasive diagnostic, cheaper than surgery. (d) Environmental: $\text{BaSO}_4$ passes through body unchanged, minimal impact.

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Water softening: $\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{CO}_3^{2-} \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3\downarrow$; precipitate; prevents \$ billions in damage; reduces detergent pollution. (2) Antacid: $\text{Mg}(\text{OH})_2 + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{MgCl}_2$; water; low-cost relief; minimal impact. (3) Barium swallow: $\text{BaSO}_4$ precipitate; insoluble $\text{BaSO}_4$; non-invasive diagnosis; $\text{BaSO}_4$ passes through unchanged. Double displacement enables practical, economic, environmental benefits.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Connecting double displacement reaction chemistry to economic and environmental applications

sections.section_title: Common Errors in Double Displacement Problems
sections.introduction: Students often make predictable errors when predicting products, checking solubility, or balancing double displacement equations. Recognizing these pitfalls and learning correction strategies builds accuracy and confidence.

key_concept.definition: Common double displacement errors include: wrong product formulas (charge balance errors), not checking solubility rules, forgetting state symbols, unbalanced equations, and confusing reaction types.
key_concept.context: A verification checklist prevents errors: products correct (charge balance)? solubility checked? states indicated? equation balanced? reaction type confirmed?

image.alt: Checklist for verifying double displacement reaction solutions
image.caption: Error prevention: check products, solubility, states, balance, reaction type.

classifications.value: Writing incorrect formulas for products due to charge balance errors
characteristics: Wrong ionic charges in product compounds
Not balancing cation and anion charges to zero
Writing element instead of compound or vice versa
classifications.behavior: Results in chemically impossible formulas. Verify product formulas: ionic compounds must have charge balance (total charge = 0). Use criss-cross method if needed.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: $\text{BaCl}_2 + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{BaSO}_4 + \text{NaCl}_2$. Correct: $\text{BaCl}_2 + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{BaSO}_4 + 2\text{NaCl}$. Na is $+1$, Cl is $-1$ $\rightarrow$ NaCl, not $\text{NaCl}_2$. Charge balance determines formula.

image.alt: Comparison of incorrect vs. correct ionic product formulas
image.caption: Ionic formula error: balance charges (Na$^+$ + Cl$^-$ $\rightarrow$ NaCl), not subscripts.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: $\text{AlCl}_3 + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{AlOH} + \text{NaCl}$. Correct: $\text{AlCl}_3 + 3\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Al}(\text{OH})_3 + 3\text{NaCl}$. Al is $+3$, OH is $-1$ $\rightarrow$ $\text{Al}(\text{OH})_3$, not AlOH. Use criss-cross: Al$^{3+}$ + OH$^-$ $\rightarrow$ $\text{Al}(\text{OH})_3$.

image.alt: Correct vs. incorrect aluminum hydroxide formula
image.caption: Formula error: Al$^{3+}$ + OH$^-$ $\rightarrow$ $\text{Al}(\text{OH})_3$, not AlOH. Balance charges.

classifications.value: Assuming reaction occurs without verifying if precipitate forms
characteristics: Assuming all double displacement reactions occur
Forgetting both products must be checked
classifications.behavior: Results in impossible reactions (e.g., $\text{NaNO}_3 + \text{KCl} \rightarrow$ products). Always check solubility of BOTH products. If both soluble, write 'no reaction'.

examples.process: Error: assuming all double displacement occurs
examples.explanation: Incorrect: $\text{NaNO}_3 + \text{KCl} \rightarrow \text{NaCl} + \text{KNO}_3$. Correct: no reaction. ALL products soluble (Group 1 + nitrate). No precipitate, water, or gas. No driving force. Always check solubility of BOTH products.

image.alt: Solubility check showing all products soluble
image.caption: Solubility error: both products soluble $\rightarrow$ no reaction. Check BOTH products.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{KCl} \rightarrow \text{NaCl}\downarrow + \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4$ (assuming NaCl precipitates). Correct: no reaction. NaCl soluble (Group 1), $\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4$ soluble (Group 1). Both soluble $\rightarrow$ no reaction. Check BOTH products.

image.alt: Checking both products for solubility
image.caption: Check BOTH products: if both soluble, no reaction occurs.

classifications.value: Forgetting to indicate states of matter (aq, s, l, g)
classifications.behavior: Omits critical information about driving force. State symbols indicate which product drives reaction (precipitate/water/gas). Always indicate states, especially (s) for precipitates.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: $\text{AgNO}_3 + \text{NaCl} \rightarrow \text{AgCl} + \text{NaNO}_3$. Correct: $\text{AgNO}_3(aq) + \text{NaCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s)\downarrow + \text{NaNO}_3(aq)$. (s) indicates $\text{AgCl}$ is precipitate driving reaction. Without it, unclear if reaction occurs.

image.alt: Comparison showing missing vs. correct state symbols
image.caption: State symbols: (s) for precipitate indicates driving force for reaction.

examples.process: Error: missing water state in neutralization
examples.explanation: Incorrect: $\text{HCl} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$. Correct: $\text{HCl}(aq) + \text{NaOH}(aq) \rightarrow \text{NaCl}(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. (l) indicates water formation drives neutralization. Important for identifying reaction type.

image.alt: Neutralization with correct state symbols
image.caption: Neutralization: $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$ indicates water formation drives reaction.

classifications.value: Writing correct products but failing to balance the equation
characteristics: Atoms not equal on both sides
Violates Law of Conservation of Mass
Forgetting to balance after predicting products
classifications.behavior: Unbalanced equations misrepresent reaction stoichiometry; cannot use for calculations. Always verify atom counts after writing products. Balance polyatomic ions as units when possible.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: $\text{AlCl}_3 + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Al}(\text{OH})_3 + \text{NaCl}$. Left: 1 Al, 3 Cl, 1 Na, 1 OH; Right: 1 Al, 3 OH, 1 Na, 1 Cl. Correct: $\text{AlCl}_3 + 3\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Al}(\text{OH})_3 + 3\text{NaCl}$. Balance Cl and OH.

image.alt: Balancing AlCl₃ + NaOH equation
image.caption: Balancing: $\text{AlCl}_3 + 3\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Al}(\text{OH})_3 + 3\text{NaCl}$; equal atoms both sides.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{KOH} \rightarrow \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$. Left: 2 H (acid) + 1 H (base) = 3 H; Right: 2 H. Correct: $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{KOH} \rightarrow \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$. Balance K and H.

image.alt: Balancing H₂SO₄ + KOH equation
image.caption: Balancing: $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{KOH} \rightarrow \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$; 2 K, 4 H, 1 S, 6 O both sides.

classifications.value: Misidentifying double displacement as single displacement, synthesis, or decomposition
characteristics: Assuming two compounds always means double displacement
Not recognizing pattern $AB + CD \rightarrow AD + CB$
Confusing with single displacement ($A + BC \rightarrow AC + B$)
Overlooking neutralization as double displacement subtype
classifications.behavior: Leads to wrong product predictions. Always check pattern: double displacement has TWO compounds → TWO compounds. One element = single displacement. One compound = decomposition.

examples.process: Error: confusing double with single displacement
examples.explanation: Incorrect: $\text{NaCl} + \text{AgNO}_3$ called single displacement. Correct: This is double displacement (compound + compound → compound + compound). Single displacement requires element + compound: $A + BC \rightarrow AC + B$.

image.alt: Comparison of double vs. single displacement patterns
image.caption: Double displacement: compound + compound → compound + compound. Single: element + compound → element + compound.

examples.process: Error: not recognizing neutralization as double displacement
examples.explanation: Incorrect: $\text{HCl} + \text{NaOH}$ called different reaction type. Correct: This IS double displacement (ion exchange): $\text{H}^+$ exchanges with $\text{Na}^+$. $\text{HCl} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$. Neutralization is double displacement subtype.

image.alt: Neutralization as double displacement
image.caption: Neutralization: $\text{HCl} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ is double displacement (ion exchange).

principles.statement: Always verify double displacement solutions by checking: (1) Products correct (charge balance?), (2) Solubility checked for BOTH products, (3) State symbols indicated ((s), (l), (g), (aq)), (4) Equation balanced (atoms equal), (5) Driving force identified (precipitate/water/gas).
principles.application: Prevents common errors; systematic verification builds accuracy habit. Use checklist for every double displacement problem.
principles.example: Problem: $\text{BaCl}_2 + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow$. Solution: $\text{BaCl}_2(aq) + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4(aq) \rightarrow \text{BaSO}_4(s)\downarrow + 2\text{NaCl}(aq)$. Check: Products correct ($\text{Ba}^{2+}$ + $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$, $\text{Na}^+$ + $\text{Cl}^-$) ✓, Solubility ($\text{BaSO}_4$ insoluble, $\text{NaCl}$ soluble) ✓, States ((s) for $\text{BaSO}_4$, (aq) for $\text{NaCl}$) ✓, Balanced (1 Ba, 2 Cl, 2 Na, 1 $\text{SO}_4$ both sides) ✓, Driving force ($\text{BaSO}_4$ precipitate) ✓.

image.alt: Checklist for verifying double displacement reaction solutions
image.caption: Verification: products, solubility, states, balance, driving force.

principles.statement: Treat polyatomic ions as single units when writing formulas and balancing equations; don't break them apart unless they decompose (like $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2$).
principles.application: $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$, $\text{NO}_3^-$, $\text{OH}^-$, $\text{PO}_4^{3-}$ stay intact in double displacement. Balance as units: $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + 6\text{NaOH} \rightarrow 2\text{Al}(\text{OH})_3 + 3\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$. Count $\text{SO}_4$ as 3 units, not 3 S + 12 O.
principles.example: Incorrect: $\text{Pb}(\text{NO}_3)_2 + \text{KI} \rightarrow \text{PbI}_2 + \text{KNO}_3$ (unbalanced $\text{NO}_3$). Correct: $\text{Pb}(\text{NO}_3)_2 + 2\text{KI} \rightarrow \text{PbI}_2 + 2\text{KNO}_3$. Balance $\text{NO}_3$ as unit (2 on both sides).

image.alt: Examples of treating polyatomic ions as units
image.caption: Polyatomic ions: treat as single units when balancing ($\text{SO}_4$, $\text{NO}_3$, $\text{OH}$, etc.).

worked_examples.title: Error Identification and Correction
worked_examples.problem: Identify the error in each double displacement attempt and provide the correct solution: (a) $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{KCl} \rightarrow \text{NaCl}\downarrow + \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4$, (b) $\text{AlCl}_3 + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{AlOH} + \text{NaCl}$, (c) $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ (unbalanced).

step_by_step.calculation: Two errors: (1) Didn't check solubility—both products soluble (Group 1 salts). (2) Wrong state (NaCl not precipitate). Correct: no reaction. Both products soluble, no driving force.

Error: wrong product formula. Al is $+3$, OH is $-1$ $\rightarrow$ $\text{Al}(\text{OH})_3$, not AlOH. Also unbalanced. Correct: $\text{AlCl}_3(aq) + 3\text{NaOH}(aq) \rightarrow \text{Al}(\text{OH})_3(s)\downarrow + 3\text{NaCl}(aq)$.

Error: unbalanced. Products correct (neutralization), but atoms unequal. Left: 3 H (2 + 1), 1 Na; Right: 2 Na, 2 H. Correct: $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4(aq) + 2\text{NaOH}(aq) \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4(aq) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. Balance Na and H.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Error: didn't check solubility, wrong state. Correction: no reaction (both products soluble). (b) Error: wrong formula ($\text{Al}(\text{OH})_3$ not AlOH), unbalanced. Correction: $\text{AlCl}_3 + 3\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Al}(\text{OH})_3\downarrow + 3\text{NaCl}$. (c) Error: unbalanced. Correction: $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$. Verification checklist prevents these errors.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Identifying and correcting common double displacement reaction errors

sections.introduction: Double displacement reaction mastery requires active practice with solubility rules application, product prediction, equation balancing, and real-world application. Work through these problems to build confidence and accuracy.

key_concept.definition: Practice problems cover: predicting if reactions occur using solubility rules, writing correct product formulas, balancing equations, identifying reaction types, and connecting reactions to applications.
key_concept.context: Active recall and immediate feedback reinforce solubility rules memorization and pattern recognition; focus on checking BOTH products for solubility, writing correct formulas, and verification steps.

image.alt: Practice strategy flowchart for double displacement reactions
image.caption: Practice strategy: exchange ions $\rightarrow$ check solubility $\rightarrow$ write products $\rightarrow$ balance $\rightarrow$ verify.

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive Double Displacement Practice Set
worked_examples.problem: Predict products and balance (write 'no reaction' if none): (a) $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{CaCl}_2 \rightarrow$, (b) $\text{KNO}_3 + \text{NaCl} \rightarrow$, (c) $\text{HCl} + \text{KOH} \rightarrow$, (d) $\text{AgNO}_3 + \text{NaBr} \rightarrow$, (e) $\text{FeCl}_3 + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow$, (f) $\text{Ba}(\text{NO}_3)_2 + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow$.

step_by_step.calculation: Exchange ions: $\text{Na}^+$ with $\text{Cl}^-$, $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ with $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$. Products: $\text{NaCl} + \text{CaCO}_3$. Check solubility: $\text{NaCl}$ soluble (Group 1), $\text{CaCO}_3$ insoluble (carbonate). Precipitate forms. Balance: $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3(aq) + \text{CaCl}_2(aq) \rightarrow 2\text{NaCl}(aq) + \text{CaCO}_3(s)\downarrow$.

Exchange ions: $\text{K}^+$ with $\text{Cl}^-$, $\text{Na}^+$ with $\text{NO}_3^-$. Products: $\text{KCl} + \text{NaNO}_3$. Check solubility: ALL Group 1 salts soluble, ALL nitrates soluble. Both products aqueous. No precipitate, water, or gas. No reaction.

Acid + base = neutralization. Exchange: $\text{H}^+$ with $\text{OH}^-$, $\text{K}^+$ with $\text{Cl}^-$. Products: $\text{KCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$. Water formation drives reaction. Balance: $\text{HCl}(aq) + \text{KOH}(aq) \rightarrow \text{KCl}(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$.

Exchange ions: $\text{Ag}^+$ with $\text{Br}^-$, $\text{Na}^+$ with $\text{NO}_3^-$. Products: $\text{AgBr} + \text{NaNO}_3$. Check solubility: $\text{AgBr}$ insoluble (Ag exception), $\text{NaNO}_3$ soluble. Precipitate forms. Balance: $\text{AgNO}_3(aq) + \text{NaBr}(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgBr}(s)\downarrow + \text{NaNO}_3(aq)$.

Exchange ions: $\text{Fe}^{3+}$ with $\text{OH}^-$, $\text{Na}^+$ with $\text{Cl}^-$. Products: $\text{Fe}(\text{OH})_3 + \text{NaCl}$. Check solubility: $\text{Fe}(\text{OH})_3$ insoluble (hydroxide), $\text{NaCl}$ soluble. Precipitate forms. Balance: $\text{FeCl}_3(aq) + 3\text{NaOH}(aq) \rightarrow \text{Fe}(\text{OH})_3(s)\downarrow + 3\text{NaCl}(aq)$.

Exchange ions: $\text{Ba}^{2+}$ with $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$, $\text{Na}^+$ with $\text{NO}_3^-$. Products: $\text{BaSO}_4 + \text{NaNO}_3$. Check solubility: $\text{BaSO}_4$ insoluble (Ba exception), $\text{NaNO}_3$ soluble. Precipitate forms. Balance: $\text{Ba}(\text{NO}_3)_2(aq) + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4(aq) \rightarrow \text{BaSO}_4(s)\downarrow + 2\text{NaNO}_3(aq)$.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3(aq) + \text{CaCl}_2(aq) \rightarrow 2\text{NaCl}(aq) + \text{CaCO}_3(s)\downarrow$. (b) no reaction (both products soluble). (c) $\text{HCl}(aq) + \text{KOH}(aq) \rightarrow \text{KCl}(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. (d) $\text{AgNO}_3(aq) + \text{NaBr}(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgBr}(s)\downarrow + \text{NaNO}_3(aq)$. (e) $\text{FeCl}_3(aq) + 3\text{NaOH}(aq) \rightarrow \text{Fe}(\text{OH})_3(s)\downarrow + 3\text{NaCl}(aq)$. (f) $\text{Ba}(\text{NO}_3)_2(aq) + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4(aq) \rightarrow \text{BaSO}_4(s)\downarrow + 2\text{NaNO}_3(aq)$. Key: always check solubility of BOTH products.

worked_examples.title: Double Displacement Reaction Identification Challenge
worked_examples.problem: Identify which are double displacement reactions and predict/balance products: (a) $\text{Zn} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow$, (b) $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{BaCl}_2 \rightarrow$, (c) $\text{CaCO}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \rightarrow$, (d) $\text{HNO}_3 + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow$, (e) $\text{Mg} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow$.

step_by_step.calculation: Not double displacement: element (Zn) + compound ($\text{HCl}$). This is single displacement: $\text{Zn} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{ZnCl}_2 + \text{H}_2$. Pattern $A + BC \rightarrow AC + B$, not $AB + CD \rightarrow AD + CB$.

Double displacement: TWO compounds ($\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{BaCl}_2$). Exchange ions: $\text{Na}^+$ with $\text{Cl}^-$, $\text{Ba}^{2+}$ with $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$. Products: $\text{NaCl} + \text{BaSO}_4$. Check: $\text{BaSO}_4$ insoluble. Balance: $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{BaCl}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NaCl} + \text{BaSO}_4\downarrow$.

Not double displacement: ONE reactant ($\text{CaCO}_3$). This is decomposition: $\text{CaCO}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2$. Pattern $AB \rightarrow A + B$, not $AB + CD \rightarrow AD + CB$.

Double displacement: TWO compounds ($\text{HNO}_3 + \text{NaOH}$). Acid + base = neutralization (double displacement subtype). Exchange: $\text{H}^+$ with $\text{OH}^-$, $\text{Na}^+$ with $\text{NO}_3^-$. Products: $\text{NaNO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$. Balance: $\text{HNO}_3 + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{NaNO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$.
Not double displacement: TWO elements (Mg + $\text{O}_2$). This is synthesis: $2\text{Mg} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{MgO}$. Pattern $A + B \rightarrow AB$, not $AB + CD \rightarrow AD + CB$.

worked_examples.final_answer: Double displacement reactions: (b) $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{BaCl}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NaCl} + \text{BaSO}_4\downarrow$, (d) $\text{HNO}_3 + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{NaNO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ (neutralization). Not double displacement: (a) single displacement (element + compound), (c) decomposition (one reactant), (e) synthesis (two elements). Key: double displacement has TWO compounds → TWO compounds.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Identifying double displacement reactions among mixed reaction types

summary: Double displacement reactions follow $AB + CD \rightarrow AD + CB$: two ionic compounds exchange ions to form two new compounds. Types: (1) Precipitation: insoluble solid forms ($\text{AgNO}_3 + \text{NaCl} \rightarrow \text{AgCl}\downarrow + \text{NaNO}_3$); (2) Neutralization: acid + base $\rightarrow$ water + salt ($\text{HCl} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$); (3) Gas-forming: gas escapes ($\text{HCl} + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightarrow \text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2\uparrow$). Solubility rules predict precipitates: always soluble (Group 1, $\text{NH}_4^+$, nitrates, acetates); soluble except ($\text{Cl}^-$, $\text{Br}^-$, $\text{I}^-$ except $\text{Ag}^+$, $\text{Pb}^{2+}$, $\text{Hg}_2^{2+}$; $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ except $\text{Ba}^{2+}$, $\text{Pb}^{2+}$, $\text{Ca}^{2+}$); insoluble except (carbonates, hydroxides except Group 1). Driving forces: precipitate (s), water (l), or gas (g) formation. If both products aqueous, no reaction. Spectator ions remain unchanged; net ionic equation omits them. Applications: water treatment (precipitation of contaminants), medicine (antacids, barium swallow), industry (pigments, soap), environmental remediation (heavy metal removal). Common errors: wrong product formulas (charge balance), not checking solubility of BOTH products, missing state symbols, unbalanced equations, confusing reaction types. Verification checklist: products correct? solubility checked? states indicated? balanced? driving force identified? Mastery enables prediction of reaction outcomes, understanding of water treatment, and recognition of double displacement in industrial and environmental applications.
